title: NOC 95104 – Labourers in rubber and plastic products manufacturing | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s manufacturing sector is prioritizing the transition to "Circular Economy" models, placing Labourers in Rubber and Plastic Products Manufacturing (NOC 95104) at the center of sustainable production. These workers are the operational support for facilities producing everything from recycled automotive parts to biodegradable medical packaging. While classified as TEER 5, this role is a strategic entry point for international workers, offering a direct path to specialized machine operation and permanent residency through localized pilot programs.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category5 (Occupations usually requiring short work demonstration)Major Group95 – Labourers in processing, manufacturing and utilitiesSub-Major Group951 – Labourers in processing and manufacturing

duties: Assisting Operators: Helping machine operators or assemblers set up and dismantle production machinery and equipment.
Material Logistics: Moving raw materials, tools, and finished products to and from work areas using hand trucks or other conveyance.
Cleaning & Maintenance: Cleaning and lubricating machinery and work areas to ensure safety and operational efficiency.
Monitoring: Watching machines for jammed materials or defective products and notifying senior operators of issues.
2026 Update: Assisting in the sorting and preparation of recycled plastic polymers for sustainable injection moulding processes.
employment_requirements: Education: Some secondary school education is usually required. No formal degree is necessary for entry-level positions.
Training: Short-term on-the-job training is provided.
Physical Demands: Ability to handle materials manually, stand for long durations, and perform repetitive motions.
Safety: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS certification and site-specific training for handling industrial chemicals and heat-press machinery.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) indicates a "Balanced" market with significant retirement openings. In regions like Mississauga and Brampton, the outlook is particularly strong as the "Plastic Valley" continues to modernize facilities with advanced automation, requiring a consistent influx of support staff to manage material flow.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook for rubber and plastic labourers is characterized by a "High Demand" for entry-level talent to support expanding production lines:
Ontario (Toronto/Mississauga): Good prospects. This region is a major manufacturing hub facing a persistent labor shortage, with more job openings than available workers to fill them.
British Columbia: Good prospects. Demand is driven by specialized manufacturing in the Lower Mainland and Vancouver Island.
Alberta: Stable demand. Opportunities are concentrated in industrial zones supporting the construction and energy sectors.
Quebec: Moderate to Limited. While Montreal has a balanced market, regional manufacturing centers maintain a steady need for replacement workers due to retirements.
Why Work in Canada as a Rubber/Plastics Labourer?
Low Barrier to Entry: Start your Canadian career without needing long-term academic credentials; most roles require only a short on-the-job demonstration.
Rapid Career Progression: 1–2 years of experience often leads to promotion into NOC 94212 (Assemblers/Finishers) or NOC 94111 (Machine Operators).
Comprehensive Benefits: Approximately 79% of workers in this NOC nationwide receive employer-funded medical, dental, and life insurance plans.
Stable Earnings: Entry-level wages are competitive, often exceeding the provincial minimums with opportunities for shift premiums.
pathways: Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): NOC 95104 is explicitly listed as an eligible occupation for provincial nomination for those with a valid job offer in Ontario.
2026 TR to PR Pathway: A landmark 2026 initiative targeting 33,000 temporary workers. Labourers in manufacturing are high-priority candidates for this fast-track transition.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): A high-success pathway in regional manufacturing centers where communities are actively recruiting for essential plant roles.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): Provides a direct route for labourers working in the manufacturing sector in specified regions of British Columbia.
